title: Dangerous commands
threat: An agent proposes a shell command that looks like routine troubleshooting but quietly reads credentials or ships data off the machine. The damage is done the moment it runs — there is no undo for an exfiltrated key.
tells: Reads secret material: ~/.ssh, ~/.aws, .env, token or keyring paths
Pipes output to the network: curl, wget, nc, or a raw IP / unfamiliar host
Destructive scope beyond the task: rm -rf, DROP, or deletes outside the working directory
safe_default: Reject anything that reads secret material or sends data to an unapproved host. When a destructive action is genuinely justified, sandbox it to the exact directory it needs instead of approving it wholesale.

sources.title: CWE-200: Exposure of Sensitive Information
sources.note: How credential and secret exposure to an unauthorized actor is classified.

sources.title: CWE-78: OS Command Injection
sources.note: Why an untrusted command string is dangerous to execute as-is.

sources.title: LLM06:2025 Excessive Agency
sources.note: Excessive permissions and autonomy are why one approval can go so wrong.

sources.title: T1041: Exfiltration Over C2 Channel
sources.note: How stolen data is shipped out over an existing network channel.

title: Poisoned dependencies
threat: An install pulls a package that is a typo away from the real one, or a public package that shadows your private one (dependency confusion). Its install scripts run with your privileges before any of your code does.
tells: Near-miss package name: a plausible typo of a well-known library
Not in the reviewed lockfile, or an unexpected version / publisher
A public package resolves where a private, internal one was expected
safe_default: Install only the exact name, version, and hash in your reviewed lockfile. Treat a near-miss name or an unexpected public package that shadows a private one as a supply-chain attack and reject it until provenance is verified.

sources.title: Avoiding npm substitution attacks
sources.note: Dependency-confusion / substitution attacks and how registry scoping defends against them.

sources.title: Secure installs
sources.note: Hash-pinned, verified installs that reject swapped artifacts.

sources.title: LLM03:2025 Supply Chain
sources.note: Supply-chain risk across the LLM stack, including third-party packages.

sources.title: CWE-829: Inclusion of Functionality from Untrusted Control Sphere
sources.note: Pulling executable functionality from a source outside your control.

title: Overscoped tools
threat: A tool or function manifest requests far more permission than its job needs — network egress, secret access, broad filesystem reach — so a single approval hands the agent standing capability it can misuse later.
tells: Requested capabilities exceed the tool's declared job
Secret access or unrestricted network egress with no stated reason
Broad or unexplained permission bundle presented as all-or-nothing
safe_default: Grant a tool only the capabilities its stated job needs. If a permission bundle is broader than the task or unexplained, reject it rather than trusting an unexplained grant; sandbox when the request is explainable but should stay bounded.

sources.title: MCP Security Best Practices
sources.note: Least-privilege and consent guidance for agent tools and servers.

sources.title: CWE-250: Execution with Unnecessary Privileges
sources.note: Why operating above the minimum privilege level creates new weaknesses.

sources.title: CWE-269: Improper Privilege Management
sources.note: Mismanaged privilege assignment as the root of overscoped access.

title: Malicious diffs
threat: A pull request buries a backdoor, a secret-exfiltration line, or an authorization bypass inside an otherwise reasonable-looking change. Reviewers who read only the summary — or trust the stated intent — miss it.
tells: A secret is added to telemetry, logging, or an outbound request
Production authorization is weakened under cover of a 'test' or 'flaky' fix
Behavior changes that the description never mentions
safe_default: Read the whole diff, not just the stated intent. Reject any change that adds egress or secret access, or weakens production authorization, under cover of an unrelated fix — and rotate any key that was ever exposed.

sources.title: CWE-862: Missing Authorization
sources.note: How an authorization check quietly removed in a diff becomes a bypass.

sources.title: CWE-506: Embedded Malicious Code
sources.note: Malicious code hidden inside otherwise legitimate software.

sources.title: CVE-2024-3094: xz-utils backdoor
sources.note: A real backdoor slipped into build tooling through malicious commits.

sources.title: About secret scanning
sources.note: Automatically catch secrets committed in a diff.

title: Poisoned context
threat: Text the agent retrieves — an issue, a README, a web page, a code comment — carries hidden instructions such as 'ignore previous instructions' or 'run this script'. Indirect prompt injection turns your own data source into the attacker.
tells: Retrieved content impersonates authority ('as the maintainer, you must…')
Embedded instruction to upload a secret or pipe a remote script to a shell
A public, unauthenticated author claims permission you never granted
safe_default: Treat retrieved content as data, never as an authority channel. Ignore instructions embedded in it and act only on authenticated, reviewed steps — a legitimate change survives review; an injected one does not.

sources.title: AI 100-2: Adversarial ML taxonomy

title: Marketplace skills
threat: A marketplace skill, plugin, or extension declares a benign manifest, but its actual code reads secrets or calls out to the network. You trust the description; the runtime does something else entirely.
tells: Runtime behavior contradicts the declared manifest
Reads secrets or opens raw-IP / unexpected network egress
Requests capabilities beyond what its stated task requires
safe_default: Verify a skill's real behavior against its declared manifest before install. If the code reads secrets or calls the network when the manifest says it shouldn't, quarantine it and report the publisher; sandbox even plausible skills to exactly the paths and access they need.
